extracted_information: The web content, 'Scaling on AWS (Part 2): > 10K Users', provides detailed architectural best practices and service recommendations for scaling applications to support over 10,000 users on AWS. It covers database options, redundancy, load balancing, and efficient content/data handling. Auto-scaling as a specific service is mentioned as being covered in a subsequent post (Part 3), not in detail here.

details: Decoupling architecture into separate tiers for horizontal scaling.
Designing a horizontally scalable architecture across Availability Zones (AZs) for increased application availability.
Using managed services whenever possible to offload administrative tasks.

Managed database services: Amazon RDS (Microsoft SQL Server, Oracle, MySQL, PostgreSQL, Aurora engines, Multi-AZ for synchronous replication and failover, automated backup, upgrades, patching, BYOL for SQL Server/Oracle); Amazon DynamoDB (fast, flexible NoSQL, consistent single-digit millisecond latency, managed, supports document/key-value store, suitable for highly non-relational data, massive data in TB range, rapid data ingest thousands of records/sec).
Self-hosted database: On Amazon EC2 for custom engines or full OS control (less recommended than managed services).

SQL vs. NoSQL guidance: SQL for well-established technology, existing code/communities, clear patterns, smaller data (not terabytes in first year); NoSQL for highly non-relational data, massive data (TB range), rapid ingest (thousands of records/sec).
Redundancy and Multi-AZ deployment for application tier.

Elastic Load Balancing (ELB): Distributes incoming traffic across EC2 instances, redundant, horizontally scaled, zero administration, supports SSL termination, sticky sessions, performs health checks.
Amazon RDS Read Replicas: For high number of read requests, available for MySQL, PostgreSQL, Aurora. Aurora Replicas use same underlying storage for minimal replication lag.

Amazon Simple Storage Service (S3): Highly durable and available object storage (videos, logs, images), serves as central static asset repository, partitions storage automatically, globally accessible via URLs.

Amazon CloudFront: Content distribution network (CDN), caches and serves frequently requested content from edge locations, serves static/dynamic content, supports SSL, zero cost for data transferred from origin.
Amazon DynamoDB: Used for storing session/state data, offloads work from primary database, fine-tune performance with provisioned throughput, fast, predictable, highly secure.

Amazon ElastiCache: Holds frequently accessed data, decreases load from primary database, protocol compliant with Redis/Memcached, managed service (detects/replaces unhealthy nodes), Redis option for replicas in different AZ for availability.

temporal_info.blog_post_context: Part 2 of a series on scaling on AWS
temporal_info.future_topics: Part 3 will cover scaling to >500K users and introduce Auto Scaling.

geographical_data.multi_az_deployment: Recommended for redundancy and availability for both application and database tiers.
geographical_data.cloudfront_global_reach: Content served from 50+ edge locations around the globe.

references.title: Scaling Up to Your First 10 Million Users (YouTube)
